Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon. Some weeks I'd put in a decent amount of hours, maybe around 24 or so, but it was scattered and chaotic. I'm a visual learner so I'd watch videos and look at diagrams, but organizing when to do that? Not my strong suit. I just kind of drifted through the course material when the mood struck me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines more than anything else. If something was due, I'd cram. If nothing was pressing, I might not touch the course for days. Regular study times sound nice in theory, but between my extracurriculars and just life stuff, I never locked into a consistent schedule. The stress would spike right before due dates and then I'd crash afterward.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or seeing a notification that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I was missing or if grades were posted. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more than I expected because they felt less boring than regular assignments. But without that external push, I probably wouldn't have logged in much at all.
Q4: For a brief period when they introduced those gamified tasks, I got into a rhythm of checking in more regularly. It was kind of surprising actually, like I found myself wanting to see my progress and earn those points or badges or whatever. But that routine didn't really stick once the novelty wore off. I'm not great at maintaining habits without something exciting keeping me hooked.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best by seeing things explained visually. I watched over two hours worth of video content because it just clicked better than reading dense text. The gamified tasks also helped because they broke things down into smaller, more manageable pieces. When I could see my progress visually, it motivated me more than just staring at a list of readings.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the discussion posts and reading materials. I only posted six times the whole course, and honestly, those felt forced. Reading long PDFs or articles just didn't stick with me, and I'd skim them at best. I knew they were probably important but I couldn't bring myself to engage with them when there were more visual or interactive options available.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was engaging, quick, or clearly connected to what would be on exams. The gamified stuff worked because it felt like I was accomplishing something tangible. If something was long and boring with no clear payoff, I'd skip it. I need that immediate sense of progress or reward, otherwise my brain just checks out.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could ignore without consequences, so I usually did ignore them. Required ones at least had the threat of affecting my grade, which gave me that push I needed. But even required stuff, if it wasn't weighted heavily, I might still skip it. The gamified tasks blurred that line a bit because they made optional stuff feel more fun and therefore more worth doing.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos and study from those on my tablet without being connected to the LMS. Sometimes I'd use YouTube to find explanations of concepts that made more sense to me visually. My notes were mostly handwritten diagrams and sketches that helped me remember things. None of that showed up in any system but it was real studying.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing downloaded materials or going over my handwritten notes, and the LMS had no idea. I'd spend hours redrawing diagrams or watching external videos that explained things better. My exam score was pretty decent at 81, but my LMS activity looked way lower than my actual effort. The system just couldn't see what I was doing offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then study it repeatedly without going back to the LMS. It was easier to have everything saved locally so I didn't need internet access every time. My resources are pretty limited so I'd go to places with wifi, download what I needed, then study at home. One download could mean hours of actual studying.
Q12: There were definitely points where I just couldn't engage anymore. When stress piled up from multiple courses and life stuff, this course would slip down my priority list. My anxiety would spike and I'd freeze up instead of being productive. I have low resilience so when things got hard, I'd just disengage rather than push through. Those weeks, my attendance and activity would tank.
Q13: Absolutely. Workload from other courses, confusion about what was actually important, and just being tired all the time made me use the LMS less. When I was overwhelmed, I'd only do the bare minimum to get by. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars also ate into my study time. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later but that rarely happened the way I planned.
Q14: Oh yeah, there were times I'd log in, click through some materials, and realize I hadn't absorbed anything. I'd be too stressed or distracted to actually focus. My eyes would be on the screen but my brain was somewhere else entirely. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though technically I was 'engaging' with the course according to the data.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is why my completion rate was so low. Even knowing something was important, I'd convince myself I'd do it later when I felt more motivated. The anxiety about starting made it easier to just avoid it altogether. I'd do other things instead, telling myself I was being productive elsewhere, but really I was just running from the stress.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. Without them, I'd float along watching videos and feeling like I was learning without actually producing anything. When a deadline approached, I'd scramble and stress out but somehow pull together decent work. My assignment scores were actually pretty good when I did submit, it was just getting myself to that point that was the struggle.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it surprised me and made me feel like maybe I could actually do this. That positive reinforcement helped my mood a lot. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose interest in improving. I needed that immediate response to stay motivated. The gamified tasks gave instant feedback which worked way better for me than waiting days for a grade.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible which sounds good but actually made it harder for me. Without strict guidelines, I'd just drift and not engage consistently. The parts that were more structured, like the gamified tasks with clear goals and rewards, kept me more on track. I needed external structure because I'm not great at creating it for myself.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier because they gave me small wins and visual progress. But the heavy reliance on discussion posts and readings made engagement harder since those don't work well for my learning style. Having eleven online courses also meant this one was competing for attention with so many others. A clearer, more visual roadmap would have helped me stay oriented.
Q20: That makes sense to me because you can click around a lot without actually doing the work that gets submitted. Maybe someone's exploring, or stressed and procrastinating by looking busy, or just confused about what's actually required. I've definitely had sessions where I was active in the LMS but not productive in terms of completing assignments. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That's kind of my situation actually. My LMS activity looks moderate but I performed reasonably well on exams and assignments when I submitted them. A lot of my real studying happened offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. You can learn effectively without constantly logging in if you're working with the content in other ways. The system just doesn't capture that.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and how long you're technically on the platform. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline studying that happens. They can't tell if you're actually focused or just have the tab open. They definitely miss the emotional and mental state behind the numbers, like whether someone's anxious or struggling.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with what you're learning, not just going through the motions. It's when the material clicks and you want to understand more, not just pass. For me, engagement happened most during the gamified tasks when I felt that spark of interest. It's about mental presence, not just physical or digital presence in a system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually learned from downloaded content or my handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture the anxiety that kept me from engaging more consistently or the stress that made me avoid things. It also wouldn't show that the gamified intervention genuinely improved my mood and motivation, even if my overall numbers still looked mediocre. There's a whole internal experience that data just can't measure.
